question: 2. A point \( P \) is taken on the curve \( y=x^{3} \). The tangent at \( P \) meets the curve again at \( Q \). Prove that the slope of the curve at \( Q \) is four times the slope at \( P \).
solution:
Solution. Let \( P \) have coordinates \( \left(x_{0}, y_{0}\right) \); then the slope at \( P \) is \( 3 x_{0}{ }^{2} \). The equation of the tangent at \( P \) is \( y=3 x_{0}{ }^{2}\left(x-x_{0}\right)+x_{0}{ }^{3} \). The points of intersection of the tangent and the original curve are determined by the relation
\[
x^{3}=3 x_{0}^{2}\left(x-x_{0}\right)+x_{0}^{3},
\]
which is equivalent to
\[
\left(x-x_{0}\right)^{2}\left(x+2 x_{0}\right)=0
\]

Hence the second point of intersection is \( \left(-2 x_{0},-8 x_{0}{ }^{3}\right) \). The slope at this point is \( 12 x_{0}{ }^{2} \), which is four times the slope at \( P \), as was to be proved.

If \( x_{0}=0 \), the tangent does not really meet the curve again. However, since the tangent in this case has a triple point of intersection with the curve, instead of the usual double point of intersection, it is reasonable to say that it meets the curve "again" at \( (0,0) \).

descriptive_long.question: 2. A point \( startpoint \) is taken on the curve \( ordinate = abscissa^{3} \). The tangent at \( startpoint \) meets the curve again at \( endpoint \). Prove that the slope of the curve at \( endpoint \) is four times the slope at \( startpoint \).
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Let \( startpoint \) have coordinates \( \left(origxcoord, origycoord\right) \); then the slope at \( startpoint \) is \( 3\,origxcoord^{2} \). The equation of the tangent at \( startpoint \) is \( ordinate = 3\,origxcoord^{2}\left(abscissa - origxcoord\right)+origxcoord^{3} \). The points of intersection of the tangent and the original curve are determined by the relation
\[
abscissa^{3}=3\,origxcoord^{2}\left(abscissa - origxcoord\right)+origxcoord^{3},
\]
which is equivalent to
\[
\left(abscissa - origxcoord\right)^{2}\left(abscissa + 2\,origxcoord\right)=0
\]

Hence the second point of intersection is \( \left(-2\,origxcoord,-8\,origxcoord^{3}\right) \). The slope at this point is \( 12\,origxcoord^{2} \), which is four times the slope at \( startpoint \), as was to be proved.

If \( origxcoord = 0 \), the tangent does not really meet the curve again. However, since the tangent in this case has a triple point of intersection with the curve, instead of the usual double point of intersection, it is reasonable to say that it meets the curve "again" at \( (0,0) \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 2. A point \( lighthouse \) is taken on the curve \( sandstone=harborage^{3} \). The tangent at \( lighthouse \) meets the curve again at \( windstorm \). Prove that the slope of the curve at \( windstorm \) is four times the slope at \( lighthouse \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Let \( lighthouse \) have coordinates \( \left(amberglow, cobblestone\right) \); then the slope at \( lighthouse \) is \( 3 amberglow^{2} \). The equation of the tangent at \( lighthouse \) is \( sandstone=3 amberglow^{2}\left(harborage-amberglow\right)+amberglow^{3} \). The points of intersection of the tangent and the original curve are determined by the relation
\[
harborage^{3}=3 amberglow^{2}\left(harborage-amberglow\right)+amberglow^{3},
\]
which is equivalent to
\[
\left(harborage-amberglow\right)^{2}\left(harborage+2 amberglow\right)=0
\]

Hence the second point of intersection is \( \left(-2 amberglow,-8 amberglow^{3}\right) \). The slope at this point is \( 12 amberglow^{2} \), which is four times the slope at \( lighthouse \), as was to be proved.

If \( amberglow=0 \), the tangent does not really meet the curve again. However, since the tangent in this case has a triple point of intersection with the curve, instead of the usual double point of intersection, it is reasonable to say that it meets the curve "again" at \( (0,0) \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question: 2. A point \( straightline \) is taken on the curve \( constanthoriz=knownvertical^{3} \). The tangent at \( straightline \) meets the curve again at \( flatplane \). Prove that the slope of the curve at \( flatplane \) is four times the slope at \( straightline \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Let \( straightline \) have coordinates \( \left(specificvertical, specifichoriz\right) \); then the slope at \( straightline \) is \( 3 specificvertical^{2} \). The equation of the tangent at \( straightline \) is \( constanthoriz=3 specificvertical^{2}\left(knownvertical-specificvertical\right)+specificvertical^{3} \). The points of intersection of the tangent and the original curve are determined by the relation
\[
knownvertical^{3}=3 specificvertical^{2}\left(knownvertical-specificvertical\right)+specificvertical^{3},
\]
which is equivalent to
\[
\left(knownvertical-specificvertical\right)^{2}\left(knownvertical+2 specificvertical\right)=0
\]

Hence the second point of intersection is \( \left(-2 specificvertical,-8 specificvertical^{3}\right) \). The slope at this point is \( 12 specificvertical^{2} \), which is four times the slope at \( straightline \), as was to be proved.

If \( specificvertical=0 \), the tangent does not really meet the curve again. However, since the tangent in this case has a triple point of intersection with the curve, instead of the usual double point of intersection, it is reasonable to say that it meets the curve "again" at \( (0,0) \).

garbled_string.question: 2. A point \( qzxwvtnp \) is taken on the curve \( wzrduhke=bnlgtiue^{3} \). The tangent at \( qzxwvtnp \) meets the curve again at \( hjgrksla \). Prove that the slope of the curve at \( hjgrksla \) is four times the slope at \( qzxwvtnp \).
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Let \( qzxwvtnp \) have coordinates \( \left(mprsjfdl, ckvdarmy\right) \); then the slope at \( qzxwvtnp \) is \( 3 mprsjfdl{ }^{2} \). The equation of the tangent at \( qzxwvtnp \) is \( wzrduhke=3 mprsjfdl{ }^{2}\left(bnlgtiue-mprsjfdl\right)+mprsjfdl{ }^{3} \). The points of intersection of the tangent and the original curve are determined by the relation
\[
bnlgtiue^{3}=3 mprsjfdl^{2}\left(bnlgtiue-mprsjfdl\right)+mprsjfdl^{3},
\]
which is equivalent to
\[
\left(bnlgtiue-mprsjfdl\right)^{2}\left(bnlgtiue+2 mprsjfdl\right)=0
\]
Hence the second point of intersection is \( \left(-2 mprsjfdl,-8 mprsjfdl{ }^{3}\right) \). The slope at this point is \( 12 mprsjfdl{ }^{2} \), which is four times the slope at \( qzxwvtnp \), as was to be proved.

If \( mprsjfdl=0 \), the tangent does not really meet the curve again. However, since the tangent in this case has a triple point of intersection with the curve, instead of the usual double point of intersection, it is reasonable to say that it meets the curve "again" at \( (0,0) \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let $n\ge 1$ and fix real numbers  
\[
a=(a_{1},\dots ,a_{n})\in\mathbf R^{\,n},\qquad d\in\mathbf R,
\qquad c_{1},\dots ,c_{n}\in\mathbf R\setminus\{0\}.
\]  
Define the cubic polynomial  
\[
f:\mathbf R^{\,n}\longrightarrow\mathbf R,\qquad 
f(x_{1},\dots ,x_{n})=\sum_{i=1}^{n}c_{i}\,(x_{i}-a_{i})^{3},
\]  
and the real hypersurface  
\[
\mathcal S=\Bigl\{(x,x_{\,n+1})\in\mathbf R^{\,n}\times\mathbf R\ \Bigm|\
           x_{\,n+1}=d+f(x)\Bigr\}.
\]

Fix a point  
\[
A=\bigl(x_{1}^{(0)},\dots ,x_{n}^{(0)},\,d+f(x^{(0)})\bigr)\in\mathcal S,
\qquad s_{i}:=x_{i}^{(0)}-a_{i}\ (\neq 0\text{ for every }i).
\]  
Put $f(A):=f(x^{(0)})=\sum_{i=1}^{n}c_{i}s_{i}^{3}$ and assume  

\[
f(A)\neq 0\qquad(\ast)
\]  
(so $A$ is not a flat point of $\mathcal S$).

Introduce the auxiliary point and line  
\[
B=(a_{1}-2s_{1},\dots ,a_{n}-2s_{n},\,d-8f(A)),\qquad
\ell=\{\,A+t(B-A)\mid t\in\mathbf R\,\}.
\]

Problems  

1. Local intersection of $\mathcal S$ with the tangent hyper-plane at $A$.  
   (a) Prove that $B\in\mathcal S$ and that $B$ lies on the tangent hyper-plane  
       $\Pi_{A}$ to $\mathcal S$ at $A$.  
   (b) Show that $\ell\subset\Pi_{A}$ and that, under $(\ast)$,
        \[
           \Pi_{A}\cap\mathcal S\cap\ell=\{A,B\},
        \]
        where the intersection multiplicity of $A$ equals $2$ and that of
        $B$ equals $1$.  Describe what happens if $f(A)=0$.

2. First-order scaling.  
   Compute the gradient $\nabla f$ and prove
   \[
      \nabla f(B)=4\,\nabla f(A).
   \]

3. Second-order scaling.  
   Let $H(P)=D^{2}f(P)$ be the Hessian of $f$ at $P$.  Show that  
   \[
      H(B)=-2\,H(A).
   \]

4. A {\em surface-centred} cubic affine self-map.  
   Define  
   \[
      \tau:\mathbf R^{\,n+1}\longrightarrow\mathbf R^{\,n+1},\qquad
      \tau(x,x_{\,n+1})=\bigl(a-2(x-a),\,d-8(x_{\,n+1}-d)\bigr).
   \]
   (a) Prove that $\tau$ sends $\mathcal S$ onto itself and that
       $\tau(\Pi_{P})=\Pi_{\tau(P)}$ for every $P\in\mathcal S$.  

   (b) Compute $\tau^{2}$ and, more generally, show that for any
       $m\in\mathbf Z$  
       \[
          \tau^{m}(x,x_{\,n+1})
          =\bigl(a+(-2)^{\,m}(x-a),\;d+(-8)^{\,m}(x_{\,n+1}-d)\bigr).
       \]

   (c) For $Y(t)=A+t(B-A)$ verify that  
       \[
          \tau\bigl(Y(t)\bigr)=
          A+(1-2t)(B-A)_{\mathrm h}+(1-8t)(B-A)_{\mathrm v},
       \]
       where ${\mathrm h}$ (resp.\ ${\mathrm v}$) denotes the first $n$
       (resp.\ the last) component of $B-A$.  Deduce in particular that
       $\tau(A)=B$, that $\ell$ is {\em not} $\tau$-invariant and that
       $\tau$ never sends $B$ back to $A$.

   (d) Show that
       \[
         D\tau(A)=\operatorname{diag}(-2,\dots ,-2,-8),\qquad
         \nabla\!\bigl(f\circ\tau\bigr)(A)=-8\,\nabla f(A),
       \]
       and recover the scaling relations of parts\,2 and\,3 from this
       differential identity.

5. (Optional, harder: global dynamics of $\tau$ on $\mathcal S$.)  
   (a) Prove that for every $(x,d+f(x))\in\mathcal S$ and every
       $m\in\mathbf Z$  
       \[
          \tau^{m}\bigl(x,d+f(x)\bigr)=
          \Bigl(a+(-2)^{\,m}(x-a),\;
                 d+(-8)^{\,m}f(x)\Bigr)\in\mathcal S.
       \]

   (b) Classify the periodic points of $\tau$ on $\mathcal S$.

   (c) Show that if $x\neq a$ then the orbit
       $\{\tau^{m}(A)\mid m\in\mathbf Z\}$ is unbounded; more precisely,
       if $f(A)\ne 0$ its Euclidean norm tends to $\infty$ as
       $|m|\to\infty$, whereas if $f(A)=0$ but $x\neq a$ the horizontal
       component diverges while the vertical one stays equal to~$d$.

   (d) Prove that $\tau$ possesses no non-trivial finite invariant
       algebraic subset of~$\mathcal S$.

\bigskip
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write $s=(s_{1},\dots ,s_{n})=x^{(0)}-a$ and
$f_{0}:=f(A)\ (\neq 0)$.

\medskip\noindent
{\bf Step 0. Two elementary identities.}
For any $u,v\in\mathbf R$
\[
u^{3}-v^{3}-3v^{2}(u-v)=(u-v)^{2}(u+2v),\qquad
(a_{i}-2s_{i})-x_{i}^{(0)}=-3s_{i}.
\tag{I}
\]

%--------------------------------------------------------------------
\medskip\noindent
{\bf 1. Local intersection.}

\smallskip
1(a) {\it The point $B$ lies both on $\mathcal S$ and on $\Pi_{A}$.}
Since $(x_{i}(B)-a_{i})=-2s_{i}$,
\[
f(B)=\sum_{i=1}^{n}c_{i}(-2s_{i})^{3}
     =-8\sum_{i=1}^{n}c_{i}s_{i}^{3}=-8f_{0},
\]
hence $B=(\dots ,d-8f_{0})\in\mathcal S$.
The tangent hyper-plane at $A$ is
\[
\Pi_{A}:\;
x_{\,n+1}=d+f_{0}+\sum_{i=1}^{n}3c_{i}s_{i}^{2}\bigl(x_{i}-x_{i}^{(0)}\bigr).
\]
Substituting $x=B$ and using (I) gives
\[
d+f_{0}+\sum_{i}3c_{i}s_{i}^{2}(-3s_{i})
=d+f_{0}-9f_{0}=d-8f_{0}=x_{\,n+1}(B),
\]
so $B\in\Pi_{A}$.

\smallskip
1(b) {\it Intersection multiplicities along $\ell$.}  
Along $\ell$ we have
\[
x_{i}(t)=a_{i}+(1-3t)s_{i},\qquad
x_{\,n+1}(t)=d+f_{0}-9tf_{0},
\]
hence
\[
f\bigl(x(t)\bigr)=(1-3t)^{3}f_{0}.
\]
Putting
\[
G(t):=x_{\,n+1}(t)-\bigl(d+f(x(t))\bigr)
     =f_{0}\bigl[1-9t-(1-3t)^{3}\bigr]=27f_{0}\,t^{2}(t-1),
\]
we see that $G$ vanishes only at $t=0$ (double root, $A$) and
$t=1$ (simple root, $B$).  If $f_{0}=0$ then $G\equiv 0$ and the whole
line $\ell$ is contained in $\mathcal S$; in that case multiplicities
collapse and $A$ is a higher-order contact point.

%--------------------------------------------------------------------
\medskip\noindent
{\bf 2. Gradient scaling.}

Because
\[
\nabla f(x)=\bigl(3c_{1}(x_{1}-a_{1})^{2},\dots ,3c_{n}(x_{n}-a_{n})^{2}\bigr),
\]
we obtain
\[
\nabla f(A)=\bigl(3c_{1}s_{1}^{2},\dots ,3c_{n}s_{n}^{2}\bigr),\qquad
\nabla f(B)=\bigl(3c_{1}(-2s_{1})^{2},\dots ,3c_{n}(-2s_{n})^{2}\bigr)
           =4\,\nabla f(A).
\]

%--------------------------------------------------------------------
\medskip\noindent
{\bf 3. Hessian scaling.}

Mixed second derivatives vanish, whereas
$\partial^{2}f/\partial x_{i}^{2}=6c_{i}(x_{i}-a_{i})$.  Therefore
\[
H(A)=\operatorname{diag}(6c_{1}s_{1},\dots ,6c_{n}s_{n}),\qquad
H(B)=\operatorname{diag}(6c_{1}(-2s_{1}),\dots ,6c_{n}(-2s_{n}))
     =-2\,H(A).
\]

%--------------------------------------------------------------------
\medskip\noindent
{\bf 4. The surface-centred cubic map $\tau$.}

\smallskip
4(a)  Let $x' := a-2(x-a)$.  Then
\[
f(x')=\sum_{i}c_{i}\bigl(-2(x_{i}-a_{i})\bigr)^{3}
      =-8f(x),
\]
hence $\tau(\mathcal S)=\mathcal S$.  For
$P=(p,d+f(p))$ write $Q=\tau(P)$.  The chain rule yields
$D\tau(P)^{T}\nabla f(Q)=\nabla\!\bigl(f\circ\tau\bigr)(P)$, which
implies $\tau(\Pi_{P})=\Pi_{Q}$.

\smallskip
4(b)  A straightforward second iteration gives
\[
\tau^{2}(x,x_{\,n+1})=
\bigl(4x-3a,\;d+64(x_{\,n+1}-d)\bigr),
\]
and by induction one finds for every $m\in\mathbf Z$
\[
\tau^{m}(x,x_{\,n+1})
=\bigl(a+(-2)^{\,m}(x-a),\;d+(-8)^{\,m}(x_{\,n+1}-d)\bigr).
\]

\smallskip
4(c)  Since $B-A=(-3s,-9f_{0})$, substituting $Y(t)$ in $\tau$ yields
\[
\tau\bigl(Y(t)\bigr)
      =\bigl(a-2s(1-3t),\,d-8f_{0}(1-9t)\bigr)
      =A+(1-2t)(B-A)_{\mathrm h}+(1-8t)(B-A)_{\mathrm v},
\]
so $\tau(A)=B$, $\ell$ is not $\tau$-invariant and $\tau$ never sends
$B$ back to $A$.

\smallskip
4(d)  The Jacobian at $A$ equals
$D\tau(A)=\operatorname{diag}(-2,\dots ,-2,-8)$, and
$f\circ\tau=-8f$ implies
\[
\nabla(f\circ\tau)(A)=D\tau(A)^{T}\,\nabla f(B)=-8\,\nabla f(A),
\]
from which parts\,2 and\,3 are recovered exactly as before.

%--------------------------------------------------------------------
\medskip\noindent
{\bf 5. Global dynamics of $\tau$ (optional).}

\smallskip
5(a) {\it Closed formula for $\tau^{m}$ on $\mathcal S$.}  
For $P=(x,d+f(x))\in\mathcal S$ one computes
\[
\tau^{m}(P)
=(\,a+(-2)^{\,m}(x-a),\;
  d-8\bigl(d+f(x)-d\bigr)+\cdots)
 =(a+(-2)^{\,m}(x-a),\;d+(-8)^{\,m}f(x)),
\]
and since $f\!\bigl(a+(-2)^{\,m}(x-a)\bigr)=(-8)^{\,m}f(x)$, the image
indeed lies on $\mathcal S$.

\smallskip
5(b) {\it Periodic points.}  
Assume $\tau^{k}(P)=P$ for some $k\ge 1$.  Writing
$P=(x,d+f(x))$, the horizontal part yields
$a+(-2)^{\,k}(x-a)=x$, that is
$\bigl((-2)^{\,k}-1\bigr)(x-a)=0$.  Because
$(-2)^{\,k}\neq 1$ for every $k\ge 1$, we must have $x=a$.
Consequently $f(x)=0$ and $P=(a,d)$.  Hence the unique periodic point
of $\tau$ on $\mathcal S$ is its fixed point $(a,d)$.

\smallskip
5(c) {\it Growth of the orbit.}  
Let $P=(x,d+f(x))\in\mathcal S$ with $x\neq a$.
Then $|x^{(m)}-a|=\lvert(-2)^{\,m}\rvert\,|x-a|$ tends to $\infty$
as $\lvert m\rvert\to\infty$.  If $f(P)\neq 0$ then the vertical
component
$\lvert x_{\,n+1}^{(m)}-d\rvert = \lvert(-8)^{\,m}f(P)\rvert$
blows up even faster, so the whole orbit escapes every compact set.
If $f(P)=0$ but $x\neq a$, the horizontal component still diverges
while the vertical one is constantly $d$; the orbit is again unbounded.

\smallskip
5(d) {\it No finite invariant algebraic subset.}  
Any $\tau$-invariant algebraic subset $Z\subset\mathcal S$ would have
to contain all iterates of each of its points.  By (b) and (c) that
forces $Z$ either to be empty or to contain the whole infinite orbit of
some non-periodic point, hence to be Zariski dense in~$\mathcal S$.
Therefore no proper finite algebraic subset of $\mathcal S$ can be
$\tau$-invariant.

\hfill$\square$

\bigskip

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimension:  the original one-variable cubic curve is replaced by an arbitrary-dimension cubic hypersurface in ℝⁿ⁺¹.  
• Additional structures:  gradients, tangent hyper-planes, intersection multiplicities, and second fundamental form all enter.  
• Extra constraints:  a unique non-trivial real point of intersection must be located and proved to be the only one.  
• Deeper theory:  the solution demands multivariable calculus, elementary algebraic geometry (multiplicity, Bezout-type counting), and differential-geometric notions (normal curvature, second fundamental form).  
• More steps:  determining Π, analysing the polynomial system, proving uniqueness, scaling of gradients, and finally scaling of curvatures—each building on the previous—makes the argument substantially longer and conceptually richer than in the one-dimensional prototype.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let n \geq  1 and fix real numbers a_1,\ldots ,a_n , d together with non-zero cubic
coefficients c_1,\ldots ,c_n.  
Put  

  S = { (x , x_{n+1}) \in  \mathbb{R}^n\times \mathbb{R} :
      x_{n+1}=d+\sum _{i=1}^{n} c_i (x_i-a_i)^3 }                    (1)

and abbreviate  

  f(x_1,\ldots ,x_n)=\sum _{i=1}^{n} c_i (x_i-a_i)^3.                         (2)

Choose a point  

  A=(x_1^{(0)},\ldots ,x_n^{(0)}, d+f(x^{(0)}))\in S , x_i^{(0)}\neq a_i,

and write s_i:=x_i^{(0)}-a_i, f(A)=\sum  c_i s_i^3.  
Denote by \Pi _A the tangent hyper-plane to the graph of g=d+f at A.
Throughout we assume  

  f(A)\neq 0                                                         (*)

(hence A is not an inflexion point of S).

Introduce  

  B:=(a_1-2s_1,\ldots ,a_n-2s_n , d-8f(A)),                              (3)

and the affine line through A and B  

  \ell ={ A+t(B-A) : t\in \mathbb{R} }.                                          (4)

Problems  

1. Local intersection (one-dimensional slice).  
   (a) Prove that B lies in S and in \Pi _A.  
   (b) Show that \ell \subset \Pi _A and that, under the hypothesis (*),

             \Pi _A\cap S\cap \ell ={A,B},                                       (5)

     with A counted with multiplicity 2 and B with multiplicity 1.  
     (Describe what changes when f(A)=0.)

2. First-order scaling.  
   Show that the gradient of f satisfies  

             \nabla f(B)=4\nabla f(A).                                        (6)

3. Second-order scaling.  
   With H(P)=D^2f(P) the Hessian matrix of f at P, prove  

             H(B)=-2H(A).                                        (7)

4. Cubic affine transformation centred at A.  
   Define  

        \tau _A : \mathbb{R}^{n+1}\to \mathbb{R}^{n+1},                                    (8)
        \tau _A(x,x_{n+1})=( a-2(x-a) , d-8(x_{n+1}-d) ).

   (a) Show that \tau _A sends S onto itself and carries each tangent
       hyper-plane \Pi _P to \Pi _{\tau _A(P)}.  

   (b) Compute \tau _A^2 and prove the formula  

             \tau _A^2(x,x_{n+1})=(4x-3a , d+64(x_{n+1}-d)).          (9)

       Hence \tau _A is not an involution; \tau _A^2 is a homothety of
       ratio 4 in the first n coordinates and of ratio 64 in the
       vertical coordinate, followed by suitable translations.

   (c) Let Y(t)=A+t(B-A) be the parametrisation of \ell .  Show that  

             \tau _A(Y(t)) = A+(1-2t)(B-A)_h + (1-8t)(B-A)_v,        (10)

       where the subscripts ``h'' and ``v'' denote, respectively,
       the first n and the (n+1)-st component of the vector B-A.
       Deduce in particular that \tau _A(A)=B, that \ell  is **not**
       \tau _A-invariant once n+1 dimensions are considered, and that
       \tau _A never sends B back to A.

   (d) Compute the differential D\tau _A(A)=diag(-2,\ldots ,-2,-8) and
       re-derive (6) and (7) from it.

5. (Optional, harder)  Let A_1,\ldots ,A_k be points of S whose first-coordinate
    vectors a^{(j)}=(a_1^{(j)},\ldots ,a_n^{(j)}) form an affine basis of \mathbb{R}^n.
    Show that the subgroup of the affine group generated by the cubic
    transformations \tau _{A_j} acts transitively on S inside each connected
    component.  
    (Hint: \tau _P\circ \tau _Q is a homothety of ratio 4 followed by a translation,
    and the translations generated under the stated hypothesis span
    \mathbb{R}^n.)
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we use (2), write s_i=x_i^{(0)}-a_i and set f_0:=f(A) (so
f_0\neq 0 by (*)).

Step 0. Two algebraic identities.  
For u,v\in \mathbb{R}  

 u^3-v^3-3v^2(u-v)=(u-v)^2(u+2v),                               (I)  
 (a_i-2s_i)-x_i^{(0)}=-3s_i.                                (II)

  
1. Local intersection.

1(a) Membership of B in S and \Pi _A.  
Since x_i(B)-a_i=-2s_i,

  f(B)=\sum c_i(-2s_i)^3=-8\sum c_i s_i^3=-8f_0,

so x_{n+1}(B)=d-8f_0, exactly its (n+1)-st coordinate.  Thus B\in S.
The tangent hyper-plane at A is

  \Pi _A : x_{n+1}=d+f_0+\sum _i 3c_i s_i^2(x_i-x_i^{(0)}).          (11)

With (II) and \sum _i c_i s_i^3=f_0 one obtains

  RHS at x=B = d+f_0+\sum _i 3c_i s_i^2(-3s_i)
             = d+f_0-9f_0=d-8f_0=x_{n+1}(B),

so B\in \Pi _A.

1(b) Intersection multiplicities on \ell .  
Along \ell  put

  x_i(t)=a_i+(1-3t)s_i, x_{n+1}(t)=d+f_0-9tf_0                (12)

and compute

  f(x(t))=(1-3t)^3 f_0.                                       (13)

Define  

  G(t):=x_{n+1}(t)-(d+f(x(t)))
       =f_0[1-9t-(1-3t)^3].                                   (14)

Expanding (1-3t)^3 gives 1-9t+27t^2-27t^3, hence

  1-9t-(1-3t)^3=27t^2(t-1).                                  (15)

Because f_0\neq 0,  

  G(t)=27f_0 t^2(t-1).                                       (16)

So G(t)=0 \Leftrightarrow  t\in {0,1}.  Therefore \Pi _A\cap S\cap \ell ={A,B}.  The factor t^2 shows
that t=0 (point A) is a double zero, whereas t=1 (point B) is simple,
establishing (5).

(Degenerate case f_0=0. Then G\equiv 0 and the whole line \ell  is contained in S;
A is an inflexion point and the ``double-simple'' description breaks
down.)

  
2. Gradient scaling (6).

\nabla f(x)=(3c_1(x_1-a_1)^2,\ldots ,3c_n(x_n-a_n)^2).

At A: \nabla f(A)=(3c_1 s_1^2,\ldots ,3c_n s_n^2).  
At B: \nabla f(B)=(3c_1(-2s_1)^2,\ldots ,3c_n(-2s_n)^2)=4\nabla f(A).

  
3. Hessian scaling (7).

Mixed derivatives vanish and \partial ^2f/\partial x_i^2=6c_i(x_i-a_i), so

  H(A)=diag(6c_i s_i), H(B)=diag(6c_i(-2s_i))=-2H(A).

  
4. The cubic affine transformation \tau _A.

4(a) \tau _A preserves S and tangent hyper-planes.  
Given x':=a-2(x-a),

  f(x')=\sum c_i(-2(x_i-a_i))^3=-8f(x),                         (17)

whence d+f(x')=d-8f(x).  Thus \tau _A(S)=S.

For P=(p,d+f(p))\in S set Q=\tau _A(P).  Writing out \Pi _P and using (6) one
verifies directly that \tau _A(\Pi _P)=\Pi _Q.

4(b) The square of \tau _A.  
The computation

  \tau _A^2(x,x_{n+1})=(4x-3a , d+64(x_{n+1}-d))                (18)

follows exactly as in the draft, proving (9).

4(c) Action of \tau _A on the line \ell .  
Let Y(t)=A+t(B-A) with B-A=(-3s,-9f_0).  Then

  \tau _A(Y(t)) = ( a-2(x(t)-a) , d-8(x_{n+1}(t)-d) )
            = ( a-2s(1-3t) , d-8f_0(1-9t) ).               (19)

Subtracting A and using B-A one gets the **componentwise** identity

  \tau _A(Y(t)) = A+(1-2t)(B-A)_h+(1-8t)(B-A)_v,              (20)

where (\cdots )_h (resp. (\cdots )_v) denotes the horizontal (resp. vertical)
part of the vector.  Consequently

  * \tau _A(A)=B, while \tau _A(B)=A+(B-A)_h-7(B-A)_v\neq A;  
  * \ell  is **not** invariant under \tau _A because the two scale factors
    1-2t and 1-8t differ unless t\in {0,\frac{1}{2}}.  
  * The first n coordinates of points on \ell  are transformed by
    t\mapsto 1-2t, whereas the (n+1)-st coordinate follows t\mapsto 1-8t.

4(d) Differential at A.  
D\tau _A(A)=diag(-2,\ldots ,-2,-8).  
Acting on the gradient (a covector) multiplies it by -2 twice, giving
the factor 4 of (6); acting on second derivatives introduces one more
-2, giving (7).

  
5. (Sketch of the optional part.)  
For P\in S write \tau _P(x)=-2x+3a_P for the first n coordinates.  A direct
computation shows

  \tau _P\circ \tau _Q (x)=4x+3(a_P-2a_Q).                              (21)

Hence any element of the subgroup G generated by the \tau _{A_j} acts on
the first n coordinates as

  x\mapsto 4^m x+u, m\in \mathbb{Z}, u\in V:=\langle 3(a^{(i)}-2a^{(j)})\rangle _\mathbb{Z}.          (22)

Because the vectors a^{(j)} form an affine basis of \mathbb{R}^n, the subgroup
V equals \mathbb{R}^n, and multiplication by 4 together with these translations
give the full affine group of \mathbb{R}^n.  Adding the vertical coordinate
(determined uniquely once the horizontal part is fixed) one sees that
G acts transitively on every connected component of S. \blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimension:  the original one-variable cubic curve is replaced by an arbitrary-dimension cubic hypersurface in ℝⁿ⁺¹.  
• Additional structures:  gradients, tangent hyper-planes, intersection multiplicities, and second fundamental form all enter.  
• Extra constraints:  a unique non-trivial real point of intersection must be located and proved to be the only one.  
• Deeper theory:  the solution demands multivariable calculus, elementary algebraic geometry (multiplicity, Bezout-type counting), and differential-geometric notions (normal curvature, second fundamental form).  
• More steps:  determining Π, analysing the polynomial system, proving uniqueness, scaling of gradients, and finally scaling of curvatures—each building on the previous—makes the argument substantially longer and conceptually richer than in the one-dimensional prototype.
